questions.prompt: Why do TLS, SSH, and PGP all use hybrid encryption (asymmetric + symmetric)?

choices.text: Asymmetric crypto is slow (RSA / EC operations take milliseconds per op); symmetric crypto is fast (AES does GB/sec on commodity hardware). For bulk data — file transfers, web traffic, messaging — symmetric is the only practical choice. But symmetric requires both parties to share a secret key, which is the hard problem. Solution: use asymmetric (slow but doesn't need pre-shared secret) to securely exchange a random symmetric key (small payload, fast even with slow crypto), then use symmetric to encrypt the bulk data. TLS: ECDH key exchange derives shared symmetric key; session uses AES-GCM. SSH: same pattern. PGP: encrypts a random session key with recipient's RSA/ECC public key; encrypts the message with that session key. Hybrid combines the strengths: asymmetric's no-pre-shared-secret property + symmetric's speed. Almost every real-world encryption protocol does this.

questions.explanation: Asymmetric for key exchange (no pre-shared secret), symmetric for fast bulk data. Best of both.

questions.prompt: Your dev uses AES-GCM but reuses the same nonce across messages with the same key. Consequence?

choices.text: Catastrophic — nonce reuse with GCM breaks both confidentiality AND authentication. Attacker can XOR two ciphertexts encrypted with the same key+nonce to learn the XOR of their plaintexts, sometimes recovering both. Worse, AES-GCM's authentication uses the nonce; reusing it lets an attacker forge messages by recovering the authentication key. There are public CVEs and academic papers on real-world systems compromised by GCM nonce reuse. Fixes: generate a fresh random 96-bit nonce per encryption (`os.urandom(12)`), or use a counter that's never reset for a given key. The nonce can be sent in plaintext alongside the ciphertext (secrecy isn't the property; uniqueness is). Some libraries make this safer with random-nonce defaults; some let you reuse and shoot yourself. Lesson: never reuse nonces. Use libraries that make it hard to misuse (e.g., libsodium's APIs).

questions.explanation: Catastrophic — breaks both encryption and authentication. Always use fresh random nonce per encryption.
